title: Haemochromatosis
oneLiner: An autosomal recessive disorder of iron overload causing progressive organ damage; presents with fatigue, arthralgia, bronze skin, and diabetes; easily treated if caught early.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** A 55-year-old man presents with fatigue and joint pain (especially knuckles and knees) for months. He has lost libido and notes his skin looks darker. On exam, you find hepatomegaly, slate-grey skin pigmentation, and spider naevi. Labs show elevated fasting glucose and mildly raised LFTs. His ferritin is >1000 ng/mL, transferrin saturation >60%.
- **Dissect the clues:**
  - *Age 40–60*: Symptoms appear after decades of iron accumulation; rarely before 40.
  - *Fatigue + arthralgia*: Most common early complaints; iron deposition in synovium causes non-erosive arthropathy (especially 2nd/3rd MCP joints).
  - *Loss of libido / impotence*: Iron deposits in pituitary (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism) — a key early clue in men.
  - *Bronze/slate-grey skin*: Iron and melanin deposition in dermis, often on face, arms, palms, and sun-exposed areas.
  - *Hepatomegaly*: Liver is main iron storage site; hepatomegaly precedes cirrhosis.
  - *Diabetes mellitus*: Iron damages pancreatic β-cells → type 1-like diabetes (brittle, insulin-requiring).
  - *LFTs*: Mildly raised transaminases (ALT/AST) — non-specific but a red flag.
  - *High ferritin + high transferrin saturation*: Pathognomonic pattern — ferritin reflects iron stores, transferrin saturation reflects iron loading.
- **Atypical presentations:**
  - *Cardiac failure*: Youngish patient (30s–40s) with dilated cardiomyopathy (restrictive physiology) and arrhythmias — iron in myocardium. Often misdiagnosed as idiopathic DCM.
  - *Abdominal pain with ascites*: Decompensated cirrhosis without prior diagnosis — check serum ferritin.
  - *Diabetes with no family history*: New-onset type 1 diabetes in an older adult (50+) — especially if no autoantibodies → think haemochromatosis.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** Haemochromatosis is a condition where your body absorbs too much iron from your diet, storing it in organs — especially the liver, heart, pancreas, skin, and joints. Over years, the excess iron causes inflammation and scarring, eventually destroying those organs.
- **Why it matters:**
  - *Frequency*: HFE-related haemochromatosis is the most common autosomal recessive disorder in Caucasians (1 in 200–300 homozygous C282Y). But only ~10–20% develop overt disease — penetrance is incomplete, so you must maintain a high index of suspicion.
  - *Stakes*: Untreated → cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma, heart failure, diabetes, hypogonadism. All are preventable with early phlebotomy.
  - *Pitfalls*: Arthralgia → dismissed as osteoarthritis; fatigue → depression; diabetes → standard management but missed iron cause. Up to 70% present first to rheumatology or endocrinology, not hepatology.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **Story of disease development:**
  - In healthy people, iron absorption is tightly regulated by the peptide hepcidin (produced by the liver). Hepcidin binds to ferroportin on enterocytes and macrophages, blocking iron release into the blood.
  - In HFE-related haemochromatosis, a mutation (usually C282Y) impairs hepcidin production — the liver does not sense iron overload correctly. Low hepcidin → ferroportin stays open → iron pours uncontrollably from gut and macrophages into plasma → transferrin becomes saturated → excess non-transferrin-bound iron (NTBI) accumulates in tissues.
  - NTBI enters cells via non-specific transporters and promotes oxidative stress → lipid peroxidation → organ damage.
- **Why each sign happens (mechanism → sign):**
  - *Arthropathy*: Iron deposits in synovial lining → inflammation → chondrocalcinosis (calcium pyrophosphate crystals) and osteoarthritis of MCPs, wrists, knees. *Mechanism:* Iron catalyses free radical formation in cartilage.
  - *Bronze skin*: Iron increases melanocyte activity (melanin) + direct iron deposition in dermis → dark, metallic hue.
  - *Diabetes*: Iron-induced pancreatic β-cell apoptosis → insulin deficiency. *Mechanism:* Oxidative stress from NTBI.
  - *Hypogonadism*: Pituitary gonadotropes are highly sensitive to iron → reduced LH/FSH → secondary hypogonadism. *Result:* loss of libido, impotence, amenorrhea, osteoporosis.
  - *Hepatomegaly and cirrhosis*: Iron in hepatocytes → fibrosis → cirrhosis. *Mechanism:* Stellate cell activation by iron-induced oxidative stress.
  - *Cardiomyopathy*: Iron in myocytes → dilated/restrictive cardiomyopathy → heart failure. *Mechanism:* Iron inhibits mitochondrial respiration → myocyte necrosis.
- **Connection to pattern recognition:** The classic vignette reflects decades of cumulative iron loading. Early symptoms (fatigue, arthralgia) are due to low-level inflammation. Later signs (diabetes, hypogonadism, cirrhosis) represent advanced organ failure. The lab pattern (high TSAT, high ferritin) directly reflects the uncontrolled iron release into blood and tissues.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue / how to think about it |
|----------|-------|-----------------------------------|
| **Hereditary (most common)** | **HFE C282Y homozygosity** (85% of cases) | Patient of Northern European descent, presenting in 5th decade, family history |
| | HFE C282Y/H63D compound heterozygote | Milder, usually needs second hit (obesity, alcohol) |
| | Other HFE mutations (S65C) | Very rare; mild phenotype |
| **Non-HFE hereditary** | Ferroportin disease (SLC40A1) | Autosomal dominant; high ferritin but *low* transferrin saturation (macrophage iron trapping) |
| | Hepcidin deficiency (HAMP, TFR2, HJV) | Juvenile haemochromatosis (age <30) — severe, often cardiac |
| **Secondary iron overload** | Multiple transfusions (thalassaemia, MDS) | Chronic anaemia, transfused every 3–4 weeks → iron loading in RES (liver, spleen, marrow) |
| | Haemolytic anaemias (sickle cell, hereditary spherocytosis) | High red cell turnover → increased iron absorption + transfusions |
| | Excessive oral iron (rare) | Supplements, especially in African/Asian populations with iron-fortified foods? Usually not pathogenic without predisposition |
| | Alcohol-related liver disease | Alcohol downregulates hepcidin → mild iron overload; ferritin may be high but TSAT normal/mildly elevated |
| | Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) | Mild iron accumulation; ferritin elevated but TSAT <45% — a confounder |
| **Neonatal haemochromatosis** | Gestational alloimmune liver disease | Liver failure in first days of life; heavy iron in hepatocytes (not due to ferroportin) |

**Most common cause:** HFE C282Y homozygosity (accounting for >80% of clinically significant disease in Caucasians).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - *HFE genetic testing:* First-line for suspected hereditary haemochromatosis. Look for C282Y and H63D mutations.
  - *Iron studies:* Transferrin saturation (TSAT = serum iron ÷ TIBC × 100%) and serum ferritin.
  - *Liver biopsy:* Reserved for those with ferritin >1000 ng/mL or elevated LFTs (to stage fibrosis). A hepatic iron index >1.9 µmol/g dry weight per year of age is diagnostic.
  - *Non-invasive fibrosis markers:* FIB-4, APRI, transient elastography (FibroScan) — increasingly replace biopsy for staging.
- **Key tests and interpretation:**
  | Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
  |------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
  | **Transferrin saturation** | Iron loading (how much iron is bound to transferrin) | First screening test — most sensitive | >45% (fasting) suggests iron loading; >60–70% strongly suggestive of haemochromatosis. Can be elevated early before ferritin rises. |
  | **Serum ferritin** | Total iron stores (plus acute phase reactant) | Measures burden of iron overload | >200 ng/mL in men, >150 in women is abnormal; >1000 → high risk of cirrhosis/death. But ferritin can be falsely elevated by inflammation, obesity, alcohol, hepatitis. |
  | **HFE genotype** | C282Y, H63D mutations | Confirms hereditary type | C282Y homozygote = diagnostic if iron overload present. Compound heterozygote (C282Y/H63D) = lower penetrance. Negative HFE → consider juvenile or secondary causes. |
  | **Liver MRI (T2* or R2* mapping)** | Hepatic iron concentration (non-invasive) | Alternative to biopsy for iron quantification | Elevated liver iron correlates with R2* relaxation rate. Useful for monitoring. |
  | **Liver biopsy (histology + iron quantification)** | Stage fibrosis, grade iron, exclude other disease | When ferritin >1000 and suspected cirrhosis | Prussian blue stain → iron in hepatocytes (periportal → panlobular). Iron index >1.9 µmol/g dry wt/yr. Fibrosis stage guides management. |
- **What single finding confirms?** HFE C282Y homozygosity plus iron overload (TSAT >45%, ferritin elevated) — no need for biopsy in most.
- **What rules out?** Normal TSAT (<20%) with normal ferritin essentially excludes significant iron overload. But early disease can have normal ferritin — TSAT is the more sensitive test.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
- **Immediate (first hour):**  
  - *No acute crisis in haemochromatosis.* The only emergency is acute iron poisoning (overdose) — not relevant. However, if the patient presents with decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, variceal bleed, hepatic encephalopathy), treat those acutely (IV terlipressin, lactulose, antibiotics).  
  - *What to start:*  
    - If glycosuria / known diabetes → check blood glucose and manage with insulin (short-acting sliding scale if needed).  
    - If cardiac failure → urgent echo and manage with diuretics (furosemide 20–40 mg IV + spironolactone 25 mg PO).  
    - *Reason:* Avoid further organ injury from hyperglycaemia or fluid overload while you organise definitive iron removal.

- **Ward (24–72 hours):**
  - **Initiate phlebotomy:** The mainstay of treatment.  
    - *Schedule:* Remove 500 mL whole blood (≈ 200–250 mg iron) once weekly or every 2 weeks, depending on baseline ferritin and tolerance.  
    - *Target:* Reduce serum ferritin to **<50 ng/mL** (normal low range) while maintaining Hb >11 g/dL.  
    - *Reason:* Phlebotomy directly removes iron-rich red blood cells → mobilises tissue iron → restorative and prevents organ progression.  
  - **Check and correct:** ECG, Hb, ferritin, TSAT after each session.  
  - **Refer to hepatology** for fibrosis assessment (FibroScan or biopsy) if ferritin >1000.  
  - **Family screening:** Offer HFE genetic testing to all first-degree relatives (siblings, children). Siblings have 1 in 4 risk of homozygosity.

- **Long-term:**
  - **Maintenance phlebotomy:** Once ferritin <50 ng/mL, space to every 3–4 months (aim ferritin <50–100 ng/mL lifelong).  
  - **Iron chelation:** If phlebotomy cannot be done (anaemia, heart failure, poor vascular access).  
    - *Deferasirox:* 20 mg/kg PO once daily (start 10 mg/kg in elderly or mild overload).  
    - *Desferrioxamine (IV/SC)*: Used when rapid chelation needed (e.g., severe cardiac iron) — 40 mg/kg/day SC over 8–12 h.  
    - *Reason:* Chelation binds iron and promotes urinary/fecal excretion. Less efficient than phlebotomy but useful for transfusion-dependent patients.  
  - **Monitor for complications:**
    - Annual LFTs, HbA1c, bone density (osteoporosis due to hypogonadism), and echocardiogram (if cardiac iron).  
    - Screen for hepatocellular carcinoma: 6-monthly ultrasound ± AFP in cirrhotic patients.  
  - **Lifestyle advice:** Avoid iron supplements, vitamin C supplements (increases iron absorption), and raw shellfish (risk of *Vibrio* infection in iron-loaded state). Limit alcohol to minimum (hepatotoxic synergy).  

- **Common pitfalls to avoid:**
  - **Do not perform phlebotomy if anaemic** (Hb <10) without first assessing cause — consider chelation instead.  
  - **Do not rely on ferritin alone to rule in/out** — inflammation can falsely elevate ferritin. Always check TSAT.  
  - **Do not check HFE genotype in everyone with high ferritin** — secondary causes (alcohol, NASH) are far more common. Only test if TSAT >45% and family history or no alternative cause.  
  - **Do not forget to screen for HCC** in cirrhotic patients — iron removal does not fully reverse cirrhosis risk of cancer.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Haemochromatosis | Alcoholic Liver Disease | Non-Alcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH) | Hereditary Hyperferritinemia-Cataract Syndrome |
|---------|-----------------|------------------------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Pain    | Arthralgia (MCPs, knees), occasional RUQ | RUQ pain if steatosis, pancreatitis | Often asymptomatic, RUQ discomfort | None |
| Exam    | Bronze skin, hepatomegaly, testicular atrophy, arthropathy | Spider naevi, palmar erythema, parotid enlargement, gynaecomastia | Hepatomegaly, acanthosis nigricans (if insulin resistance) | Bilateral cataracts (early onset, juvenile) |
| Lab: Fe/ferritin | **High TSAT >45%**, high ferritin | Ferritin high but **TSAT normal** (<45%) | Ferritin high but TSAT normal (unless coexisting HFE) | Very high ferritin (often >1000), **TSAT normal** |
| LFTs    | Mildly elevated ALT/AST | AST:ALT >2:1 (heavy drinkers) | ALT > AST (mild, <5x ULN) | Normal |
| Genetic | HFE C282Y +/- H63D | No specific mutation; history of alcohol | No specific mutation; PNPLA3 risk | **L-ferritin gene (FTL)** mutation → ferritin without iron overload |
| Key diff | TSAT >45% + HFE mutation | History, GGT, MCV >100 | Ultrasound shows steatosis, biopsy shows steatohepatitis | Cataracts + normal iron studies + high ferritin → check FTL gene |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC):**  
  *Presentation:* Ascites, variceal bleeding, jaundice, or incidental liver nodule on ultrasound. `Key signs`: spider naevi, caput medusae, asterixis. `What to do`: Screen every 6 months with US + AFP. Refer for transplant if cirrhosis decompensated or early HCC.  
  *Late pattern:* "Patient with unexplained cirrhosis who drank minimally" — check ferritin.

- **Diabetes mellitus (bronze diabetes):**  
  *Presentation:* High blood glucose, often ketosis-prone, insulin-requiring from diagnosis. `Key signs`: May coexist with neuropathy or retinopathy (but less common than type 2). `What to do`: Start insulin, monitor HbA1c, but the real treatment is phlebotomy — glycaemic control improves with iron removal.

- **Cardiomyopathy (iron overload heart disease):**  
  *Presentation:* Dyspnoea, pedal oedema, arrhythmias (atrial fibrillation, ventricular ectopy). `Key signs`: Clinical heart failure with dilated cardiomyopathy (sometimes restrictive pattern). `What to do`: Echocardiogram (low ejection fraction), MRI with T2* mapping (normal >20 ms; severe overload <10 ms). Treat with aggressive phlebotomy + chelation (deferasirox or desferrioxamine). Do not forget iron removal in heart failure — it can be reversible.

- **Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism:**  
  *Presentation:* Loss of libido, erectile dysfunction, amenorrhea, osteoporosis, delayed puberty if juvenile onset. `Key signs`: Low LH/FSH, low testosterone, normal prolactin. `What to do`: Hormone replacement (testosterone gel or IM; estrogen + progesterone in women). Bone density screening.

- **Arthritis (chondrocalcinosis / osteoarthritis):**  
  *Presentation:* Chronic pain and stiffness in MCPs, wrists, knees. X-ray may show chondrocalcinosis (linear calcium deposits in knee menisci or wrist triangular fibrocartilage). `What to do`: NSAIDs, but phlebotomy does not reverse existing arthropathy (prevents progression). Misdiagnosed as OA → delayed treatment.

- **Infections (increased susceptibility to *Vibrio vulnificus* and *Yersinia enterocolitica*):**  
  *Presentation:* After eating raw oysters (Vibrio) — severe necrotising fasciitis or sepsis. `Key sign`: Iron-loaded macrophages impair intracellular killing. `What to do`: Early antibiotics, surgical debridement.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic for clinical features of haemochromatosis: "ABCD"**  
  - **A** – Arthropathy (especially MCPs)  
  - **B** – Bronze skin  
  - **C** – Cirrhosis / Cardiomyopathy  
  - **D** – Diabetes / Hypogonadism  
- **Mnemonic for causes of secondary iron overload: "IRON"**  
  - **I** – Intravenous (transfusions)  
  - **R** – Red cell turnover (haemolysis)  
  - **O** – Oral supplements (excessive, rare)  
  - **N** – NASH / Alcohol  
- **Clinical pearls:**
  1. *First test is transferrin saturation, not ferritin.* Ferritin is acute phase — TSAT reflects iron loading early.
  2. *If a man with "idiopathic" arthritis has normal X-rays and involves the 2nd and 3rd MCPs, think haemochromatosis.*
  3. *In any older patient with new-onset diabetes and LFT derangement, check iron studies.*
  4. *Penetrance is only ~1 in 5 C282Y homozygotes develop disease — so don't overdiagnose, but don't miss those who do.*
  5. *Phlebotomy is safer and more effective than chelation — always first line if patient can tolerate.*
  6. *Vitamin C increases iron absorption — warn patients to avoid high-dose supplements.*
  7. *Annual surveillance for HCC is mandatory even after iron correction — cirrhosis risk persists.*
- **Common mistakes:**
  - Ordering HFE genotype on everyone with high ferritin without checking TSAT. You'll find many false positives (NASH with elevated ferritin).
  - Forgetting to screen family. This is a *preventable* autosomal recessive condition — siblings and children need testing.
  - Assuming phlebotomy is only for hematologists. As an FY1, you can initiate weekly phlebotomy with a simple order (500 mL blood removal, check Hb before each). Get help from phlebotomy team or day unit.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / on-call hepatology if:**
  - Ferritin >1000 ng/mL — needs urgent assessment for fibrosis and possible biopsy.
  - Decompensated liver disease (ascites, jaundice, encephalopathy). Use Child-Pugh score; escalate to transplant team.
  - High suspicion of HCC (on ultrasound or rising AFP) — refer for urgent MDT.
  - Cardiac involvement suspected (low LVEF, arrhythmia) — involve cardiology with expertise in iron overload.
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - **NEWS2 score ≥ 7** or any single red parameter (e.g., RR ≤5 or ≥30, HR ≤40 or ≥130, systolic BP <90 mmHg).
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** — think septic shock from *Vibrio* or *Yersinia*.
  - **GCS ≤12** — hepatic encephalopathy or hypoglycaemia (check glucose).
  - **Cardiac arrest or life-threatening arrhythmia** — treat per ALS.
  - **Acute variceal haemorrhage** (haematemesis, melaena, haemodynamic instability) — activate massive transfusion protocol, urgent endoscopy.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - *Platelets <50 x 10⁹/L* in cirrhotic patient → risk of variceal bleeding; consider referral for TIPS or beta-blocker prophylaxis.
  - *INR >2.0* → severe liver dysfunction; escalate.
  - *Haemoglobin <8 g/dL* pre-phlebotomy → pause phlebotomy; consider chelation if overload persists.
  - *Transferrin saturation >90%* with rapid progression → consider juvenile haemochromatosis (HAMP/HJV) — consult genetics.

---

*Study hard — this disease is one of the few in medicine where a simple blood test and phlebotomy can entirely prevent end-stage organ failure. You'll never regret knowing it well.*

rawMarkdown:
## Haemochromatosis
**One-liner:** An autosomal recessive disorder of iron overload causing progressive organ damage; presents with fatigue, arthralgia, bronze skin, and diabetes; easily treated if caught early.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
- **Classic vignette:** A 55-year-old man presents with fatigue and joint pain (especially knuckles and knees) for months. He has lost libido and notes his skin looks darker. On exam, you find hepatomegaly, slate-grey skin pigmentation, and spider naevi. Labs show elevated fasting glucose and mildly raised LFTs. His ferritin is >1000 ng/mL, transferrin saturation >60%.
- **Dissect the clues:**
  - *Age 40–60*: Symptoms appear after decades of iron accumulation; rarely before 40.
  - *Fatigue + arthralgia*: Most common early complaints; iron deposition in synovium causes non-erosive arthropathy (especially 2nd/3rd MCP joints).
  - *Loss of libido / impotence*: Iron deposits in pituitary (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism) — a key early clue in men.
  - *Bronze/slate-grey skin*: Iron and melanin deposition in dermis, often on face, arms, palms, and sun-exposed areas.
  - *Hepatomegaly*: Liver is main iron storage site; hepatomegaly precedes cirrhosis.
  - *Diabetes mellitus*: Iron damages pancreatic β-cells → type 1-like diabetes (brittle, insulin-requiring).
  - *LFTs*: Mildly raised transaminases (ALT/AST) — non-specific but a red flag.
  - *High ferritin + high transferrin saturation*: Pathognomonic pattern — ferritin reflects iron stores, transferrin saturation reflects iron loading.
- **Atypical presentations:**
  - *Cardiac failure*: Youngish patient (30s–40s) with dilated cardiomyopathy (restrictive physiology) and arrhythmias — iron in myocardium. Often misdiagnosed as idiopathic DCM.
  - *Abdominal pain with ascites*: Decompensated cirrhosis without prior diagnosis — check serum ferritin.
  - *Diabetes with no family history*: New-onset type 1 diabetes in an older adult (50+) — especially if no autoantibodies → think haemochromatosis.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
- **Plain-language definition:** Haemochromatosis is a condition where your body absorbs too much iron from your diet, storing it in organs — especially the liver, heart, pancreas, skin, and joints. Over years, the excess iron causes inflammation and scarring, eventually destroying those organs.
- **Why it matters:**
  - *Frequency*: HFE-related haemochromatosis is the most common autosomal recessive disorder in Caucasians (1 in 200–300 homozygous C282Y). But only ~10–20% develop overt disease — penetrance is incomplete, so you must maintain a high index of suspicion.
  - *Stakes*: Untreated → cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma, heart failure, diabetes, hypogonadism. All are preventable with early phlebotomy.
  - *Pitfalls*: Arthralgia → dismissed as osteoarthritis; fatigue → depression; diabetes → standard management but missed iron cause. Up to 70% present first to rheumatology or endocrinology, not hepatology.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
- **Story of disease development:**
  - In healthy people, iron absorption is tightly regulated by the peptide hepcidin (produced by the liver). Hepcidin binds to ferroportin on enterocytes and macrophages, blocking iron release into the blood.
  - In HFE-related haemochromatosis, a mutation (usually C282Y) impairs hepcidin production — the liver does not sense iron overload correctly. Low hepcidin → ferroportin stays open → iron pours uncontrollably from gut and macrophages into plasma → transferrin becomes saturated → excess non-transferrin-bound iron (NTBI) accumulates in tissues.
  - NTBI enters cells via non-specific transporters and promotes oxidative stress → lipid peroxidation → organ damage.
- **Why each sign happens (mechanism → sign):**
  - *Arthropathy*: Iron deposits in synovial lining → inflammation → chondrocalcinosis (calcium pyrophosphate crystals) and osteoarthritis of MCPs, wrists, knees. *Mechanism:* Iron catalyses free radical formation in cartilage.
  - *Bronze skin*: Iron increases melanocyte activity (melanin) + direct iron deposition in dermis → dark, metallic hue.
  - *Diabetes*: Iron-induced pancreatic β-cell apoptosis → insulin deficiency. *Mechanism:* Oxidative stress from NTBI.
  - *Hypogonadism*: Pituitary gonadotropes are highly sensitive to iron → reduced LH/FSH → secondary hypogonadism. *Result:* loss of libido, impotence, amenorrhea, osteoporosis.
  - *Hepatomegaly and cirrhosis*: Iron in hepatocytes → fibrosis → cirrhosis. *Mechanism:* Stellate cell activation by iron-induced oxidative stress.
  - *Cardiomyopathy*: Iron in myocytes → dilated/restrictive cardiomyopathy → heart failure. *Mechanism:* Iron inhibits mitochondrial respiration → myocyte necrosis.
- **Connection to pattern recognition:** The classic vignette reflects decades of cumulative iron loading. Early symptoms (fatigue, arthralgia) are due to low-level inflammation. Later signs (diabetes, hypogonadism, cirrhosis) represent advanced organ failure. The lab pattern (high TSAT, high ferritin) directly reflects the uncontrolled iron release into blood and tissues.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Cause | Stem clue / how to think about it |
|----------|-------|-----------------------------------|
| **Hereditary (most common)** | **HFE C282Y homozygosity** (85% of cases) | Patient of Northern European descent, presenting in 5th decade, family history |
| | HFE C282Y/H63D compound heterozygote | Milder, usually needs second hit (obesity, alcohol) |
| | Other HFE mutations (S65C) | Very rare; mild phenotype |
| **Non-HFE hereditary** | Ferroportin disease (SLC40A1) | Autosomal dominant; high ferritin but *low* transferrin saturation (macrophage iron trapping) |
| | Hepcidin deficiency (HAMP, TFR2, HJV) | Juvenile haemochromatosis (age <30) — severe, often cardiac |
| **Secondary iron overload** | Multiple transfusions (thalassaemia, MDS) | Chronic anaemia, transfused every 3–4 weeks → iron loading in RES (liver, spleen, marrow) |
| | Haemolytic anaemias (sickle cell, hereditary spherocytosis) | High red cell turnover → increased iron absorption + transfusions |
| | Excessive oral iron (rare) | Supplements, especially in African/Asian populations with iron-fortified foods? Usually not pathogenic without predisposition |
| | Alcohol-related liver disease | Alcohol downregulates hepcidin → mild iron overload; ferritin may be high but TSAT normal/mildly elevated |
| | Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) | Mild iron accumulation; ferritin elevated but TSAT <45% — a confounder |
| **Neonatal haemochromatosis** | Gestational alloimmune liver disease | Liver failure in first days of life; heavy iron in hepatocytes (not due to ferroportin) |

**Most common cause:** HFE C282Y homozygosity (accounting for >80% of clinically significant disease in Caucasians).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - *HFE genetic testing:* First-line for suspected hereditary haemochromatosis. Look for C282Y and H63D mutations.
  - *Iron studies:* Transferrin saturation (TSAT = serum iron ÷ TIBC × 100%) and serum ferritin.
  - *Liver biopsy:* Reserved for those with ferritin >1000 ng/mL or elevated LFTs (to stage fibrosis). A hepatic iron index >1.9 µmol/g dry weight per year of age is diagnostic.
  - *Non-invasive fibrosis markers:* FIB-4, APRI, transient elastography (FibroScan) — increasingly replace biopsy for staging.
- **Key tests and interpretation:**
  | Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
  |------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
  | **Transferrin saturation** | Iron loading (how much iron is bound to transferrin) | First screening test — most sensitive | >45% (fasting) suggests iron loading; >60–70% strongly suggestive of haemochromatosis. Can be elevated early before ferritin rises. |
  | **Serum ferritin** | Total iron stores (plus acute phase reactant) | Measures burden of iron overload | >200 ng/mL in men, >150 in women is abnormal; >1000 → high risk of cirrhosis/death. But ferritin can be falsely elevated by inflammation, obesity, alcohol, hepatitis. |
  | **HFE genotype** | C282Y, H63D mutations | Confirms hereditary type | C282Y homozygote = diagnostic if iron overload present. Compound heterozygote (C282Y/H63D) = lower penetrance. Negative HFE → consider juvenile or secondary causes. |
  | **Liver MRI (T2* or R2* mapping)** | Hepatic iron concentration (non-invasive) | Alternative to biopsy for iron quantification | Elevated liver iron correlates with R2* relaxation rate. Useful for monitoring. |
  | **Liver biopsy (histology + iron quantification)** | Stage fibrosis, grade iron, exclude other disease | When ferritin >1000 and suspected cirrhosis | Prussian blue stain → iron in hepatocytes (periportal → panlobular). Iron index >1.9 µmol/g dry wt/yr. Fibrosis stage guides management. |
- **What single finding confirms?** HFE C282Y homozygosity plus iron overload (TSAT >45%, ferritin elevated) — no need for biopsy in most.
- **What rules out?** Normal TSAT (<20%) with normal ferritin essentially excludes significant iron overload. But early disease can have normal ferritin — TSAT is the more sensitive test.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
- **Immediate (first hour):**  
  - *No acute crisis in haemochromatosis.* The only emergency is acute iron poisoning (overdose) — not relevant. However, if the patient presents with decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, variceal bleed, hepatic encephalopathy), treat those acutely (IV terlipressin, lactulose, antibiotics).  
  - *What to start:*  
    - If glycosuria / known diabetes → check blood glucose and manage with insulin (short-acting sliding scale if needed).  
    - If cardiac failure → urgent echo and manage with diuretics (furosemide 20–40 mg IV + spironolactone 25 mg PO).  
    - *Reason:* Avoid further organ injury from hyperglycaemia or fluid overload while you organise definitive iron removal.

- **Ward (24–72 hours):**
  - **Initiate phlebotomy:** The mainstay of treatment.  
    - *Schedule:* Remove 500 mL whole blood (≈ 200–250 mg iron) once weekly or every 2 weeks, depending on baseline ferritin and tolerance.  
    - *Target:* Reduce serum ferritin to **<50 ng/mL** (normal low range) while maintaining Hb >11 g/dL.  
    - *Reason:* Phlebotomy directly removes iron-rich red blood cells → mobilises tissue iron → restorative and prevents organ progression.  
  - **Check and correct:** ECG, Hb, ferritin, TSAT after each session.  
  - **Refer to hepatology** for fibrosis assessment (FibroScan or biopsy) if ferritin >1000.  
  - **Family screening:** Offer HFE genetic testing to all first-degree relatives (siblings, children). Siblings have 1 in 4 risk of homozygosity.

- **Long-term:**
  - **Maintenance phlebotomy:** Once ferritin <50 ng/mL, space to every 3–4 months (aim ferritin <50–100 ng/mL lifelong).  
  - **Iron chelation:** If phlebotomy cannot be done (anaemia, heart failure, poor vascular access).  
    - *Deferasirox:* 20 mg/kg PO once daily (start 10 mg/kg in elderly or mild overload).  
    - *Desferrioxamine (IV/SC)*: Used when rapid chelation needed (e.g., severe cardiac iron) — 40 mg/kg/day SC over 8–12 h.  
    - *Reason:* Chelation binds iron and promotes urinary/fecal excretion. Less efficient than phlebotomy but useful for transfusion-dependent patients.  
  - **Monitor for complications:**
    - Annual LFTs, HbA1c, bone density (osteoporosis due to hypogonadism), and echocardiogram (if cardiac iron).  
    - Screen for hepatocellular carcinoma: 6-monthly ultrasound ± AFP in cirrhotic patients.  
  - **Lifestyle advice:** Avoid iron supplements, vitamin C supplements (increases iron absorption), and raw shellfish (risk of *Vibrio* infection in iron-loaded state). Limit alcohol to minimum (hepatotoxic synergy).  

- **Common pitfalls to avoid:**
  - **Do not perform phlebotomy if anaemic** (Hb <10) without first assessing cause — consider chelation instead.  
  - **Do not rely on ferritin alone to rule in/out** — inflammation can falsely elevate ferritin. Always check TSAT.  
  - **Do not check HFE genotype in everyone with high ferritin** — secondary causes (alcohol, NASH) are far more common. Only test if TSAT >45% and family history or no alternative cause.  
  - **Do not forget to screen for HCC** in cirrhotic patients — iron removal does not fully reverse cirrhosis risk of cancer.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Haemochromatosis | Alcoholic Liver Disease | Non-Alcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH) | Hereditary Hyperferritinemia-Cataract Syndrome |
|---------|-----------------|------------------------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Pain    | Arthralgia (MCPs, knees), occasional RUQ | RUQ pain if steatosis, pancreatitis | Often asymptomatic, RUQ discomfort | None |
| Exam    | Bronze skin, hepatomegaly, testicular atrophy, arthropathy | Spider naevi, palmar erythema, parotid enlargement, gynaecomastia | Hepatomegaly, acanthosis nigricans (if insulin resistance) | Bilateral cataracts (early onset, juvenile) |
| Lab: Fe/ferritin | **High TSAT >45%**, high ferritin | Ferritin high but **TSAT normal** (<45%) | Ferritin high but TSAT normal (unless coexisting HFE) | Very high ferritin (often >1000), **TSAT normal** |
| LFTs    | Mildly elevated ALT/AST | AST:ALT >2:1 (heavy drinkers) | ALT > AST (mild, <5x ULN) | Normal |
| Genetic | HFE C282Y +/- H63D | No specific mutation; history of alcohol | No specific mutation; PNPLA3 risk | **L-ferritin gene (FTL)** mutation → ferritin without iron overload |
| Key diff | TSAT >45% + HFE mutation | History, GGT, MCV >100 | Ultrasound shows steatosis, biopsy shows steatohepatitis | Cataracts + normal iron studies + high ferritin → check FTL gene |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC):**  
  *Presentation:* Ascites, variceal bleeding, jaundice, or incidental liver nodule on ultrasound. `Key signs`: spider naevi, caput medusae, asterixis. `What to do`: Screen every 6 months with US + AFP. Refer for transplant if cirrhosis decompensated or early HCC.  
  *Late pattern:* "Patient with unexplained cirrhosis who drank minimally" — check ferritin.

- **Diabetes mellitus (bronze diabetes):**  
  *Presentation:* High blood glucose, often ketosis-prone, insulin-requiring from diagnosis. `Key signs`: May coexist with neuropathy or retinopathy (but less common than type 2). `What to do`: Start insulin, monitor HbA1c, but the real treatment is phlebotomy — glycaemic control improves with iron removal.

- **Cardiomyopathy (iron overload heart disease):**  
  *Presentation:* Dyspnoea, pedal oedema, arrhythmias (atrial fibrillation, ventricular ectopy). `Key signs`: Clinical heart failure with dilated cardiomyopathy (sometimes restrictive pattern). `What to do`: Echocardiogram (low ejection fraction), MRI with T2* mapping (normal >20 ms; severe overload <10 ms). Treat with aggressive phlebotomy + chelation (deferasirox or desferrioxamine). Do not forget iron removal in heart failure — it can be reversible.

- **Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism:**  
  *Presentation:* Loss of libido, erectile dysfunction, amenorrhea, osteoporosis, delayed puberty if juvenile onset. `Key signs`: Low LH/FSH, low testosterone, normal prolactin. `What to do`: Hormone replacement (testosterone gel or IM; estrogen + progesterone in women). Bone density screening.

- **Arthritis (chondrocalcinosis / osteoarthritis):**  
  *Presentation:* Chronic pain and stiffness in MCPs, wrists, knees. X-ray may show chondrocalcinosis (linear calcium deposits in knee menisci or wrist triangular fibrocartilage). `What to do`: NSAIDs, but phlebotomy does not reverse existing arthropathy (prevents progression). Misdiagnosed as OA → delayed treatment.

- **Infections (increased susceptibility to *Vibrio vulnificus* and *Yersinia enterocolitica*):**  
  *Presentation:* After eating raw oysters (Vibrio) — severe necrotising fasciitis or sepsis. `Key sign`: Iron-loaded macrophages impair intracellular killing. `What to do`: Early antibiotics, surgical debridement.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
- **Mnemonic for clinical features of haemochromatosis: "ABCD"**  
  - **A** – Arthropathy (especially MCPs)  
  - **B** – Bronze skin  
  - **C** – Cirrhosis / Cardiomyopathy  
  - **D** – Diabetes / Hypogonadism  
- **Mnemonic for causes of secondary iron overload: "IRON"**  
  - **I** – Intravenous (transfusions)  
  - **R** – Red cell turnover (haemolysis)  
  - **O** – Oral supplements (excessive, rare)  
  - **N** – NASH / Alcohol  
- **Clinical pearls:**
  1. *First test is transferrin saturation, not ferritin.* Ferritin is acute phase — TSAT reflects iron loading early.
  2. *If a man with "idiopathic" arthritis has normal X-rays and involves the 2nd and 3rd MCPs, think haemochromatosis.*
  3. *In any older patient with new-onset diabetes and LFT derangement, check iron studies.*
  4. *Penetrance is only ~1 in 5 C282Y homozygotes develop disease — so don't overdiagnose, but don't miss those who do.*
  5. *Phlebotomy is safer and more effective than chelation — always first line if patient can tolerate.*
  6. *Vitamin C increases iron absorption — warn patients to avoid high-dose supplements.*
  7. *Annual surveillance for HCC is mandatory even after iron correction — cirrhosis risk persists.*
- **Common mistakes:**
  - Ordering HFE genotype on everyone with high ferritin without checking TSAT. You'll find many false positives (NASH with elevated ferritin).
  - Forgetting to screen family. This is a *preventable* autosomal recessive condition — siblings and children need testing.
  - Assuming phlebotomy is only for hematologists. As an FY1, you can initiate weekly phlebotomy with a simple order (500 mL blood removal, check Hb before each). Get help from phlebotomy team or day unit.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / on-call hepatology if:**
  - Ferritin >1000 ng/mL — needs urgent assessment for fibrosis and possible biopsy.
  - Decompensated liver disease (ascites, jaundice, encephalopathy). Use Child-Pugh score; escalate to transplant team.
  - High suspicion of HCC (on ultrasound or rising AFP) — refer for urgent MDT.
  - Cardiac involvement suspected (low LVEF, arrhythmia) — involve cardiology with expertise in iron overload.
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - **NEWS2 score ≥ 7** or any single red parameter (e.g., RR ≤5 or ≥30, HR ≤40 or ≥130, systolic BP <90 mmHg).
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** — think septic shock from *Vibrio* or *Yersinia*.
  - **GCS ≤12** — hepatic encephalopathy or hypoglycaemia (check glucose).
  - **Cardiac arrest or life-threatening arrhythmia** — treat per ALS.
  - **Acute variceal haemorrhage** (haematemesis, melaena, haemodynamic instability) — activate massive transfusion protocol, urgent endoscopy.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - *Platelets <50 x 10⁹/L* in cirrhotic patient → risk of variceal bleeding; consider referral for TIPS or beta-blocker prophylaxis.
  - *INR >2.0* → severe liver dysfunction; escalate.
  - *Haemoglobin <8 g/dL* pre-phlebotomy → pause phlebotomy; consider chelation if overload persists.
  - *Transferrin saturation >90%* with rapid progression → consider juvenile haemochromatosis (HAMP/HJV) — consult genetics.

---

*Study hard — this disease is one of the few in medicine where a simple blood test and phlebotomy can entirely prevent end-stage organ failure. You'll never regret knowing it well.*
